title: Quick check: reset password and MFA

questions.prompt: Why are password reset and MFA reset grouped as a single playbook step?
choices: Resetting the password without resetting MFA leaves the attacker with a foothold to recover the new password via password-reset flows that depend on MFA factors they still control.

choices.label: They are combined for billing efficiency.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: MFA reset must always happen before password reset technically.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour; they happen together.

choices.label: They produce the same audit-log entry, so they are grouped for documentation.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the audit shape.

questions.explanation: The password closes the front door. The MFA reset removes attacker-added second factors that could be used to recover the new password through reset flows.

questions.prompt: Why is emailing the new password to the user's mailbox a cardinal mistake?

choices.label: Email is too slow as a delivery channel.
choices.rationale: Speed is not the issue. Channel compromise is.

choices: The mailbox may still be compromised via OAuth tokens or other persistence, so the new credential lands where the attacker can read it.

choices.label: Email passwords are filtered out by spam protection.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: The customer's IT manager would object.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing. The reason is channel security.

questions.explanation: The mailbox is potentially compromised. The new password must travel through a channel the attacker provably cannot access.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson prefer auto-generated strong passwords over operator-chosen memorable ones?
choices: Auto-generation removes the operator-chosen-weak-password risk. Memorable or pattern-based passwords are predictable.

choices.label: Auto-generation is faster.
choices.rationale: Speed is not the framing; security is.

choices.label: Auto-generated passwords are required by the platform.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform requirement.

choices.label: Memorable passwords get rejected by the identity provider.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Humans pick weak, patterned passwords. Auto-generation produces strong credentials by default.

questions.prompt: You have completed session revoke on Martin. He is at his desk, reachable, waiting for the credential handoff. How do you generate and deliver the credential?

choices.label: Generate a memorable password like Globex2026! and email it to Martin's mailbox.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Mailbox may still be compromised; memorable passwords are weak.

choices.label: Tell Martin the new password verbally over the phone call.
choices.rationale: Voice channels can be recorded or overheard. Phone is fine for identity verification, not for the password itself.

choices: Use the portal's auto-generated strong password. Push it to your MSP's secure-credential handoff tool (e.g. a single-use 1Password Send link). Call Martin on his documented number to confirm identity and walk him through accessing the credential. Document the method and timestamp.

choices.label: Let Martin choose his own password and type it in.
choices.rationale: Operator-chosen passwords are predictable and may be overheard.

questions.explanation: Strong credential, secure channel, identity-verified delivery, full documentation. The handoff tool keeps the password out of any channel the attacker may still control.

questions.prompt: Martin signs in and is prompted to enrol MFA. He says: "Can you leave my old MFA in place? I have a meeting in five minutes." What do you do?

choices.label: Tell him "yes, you can use your old MFA, we will not enforce re-enrolment."
choices.rationale: The old MFA methods may include attacker-added entries. Re-enrolment is part of the response.

choices: Explain that re-enrolment is necessary because one of the old methods may have been added by the attacker. Offer a 90-second walk-through. Offer a fallback if he genuinely does not have time, but do not skip re-enrolment.

choices.label: Tell him to figure it out himself and end the call.
choices.rationale: Wrong tone. Abandoning him at the moment of friction is a customer-relationship failure.

questions.explanation: Name why re-enrolment is necessary. Offer a fast walk-through. Give a fallback that does not compromise the response. The old MFA methods may include attacker-added entries.

questions.prompt: "Resetting the password but not the MFA" leaves what specific consequence?

choices.label: Nothing. Password reset alone is enough.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The lesson is explicit about why both are needed.

choices: The attacker may have added their own MFA method. Future password-recovery flows would route to the attacker's MFA, letting them recover the new password.

choices.label: The audit log shows incomplete remediation, but there is no real consequence.
choices.rationale: Audit appearance is not the issue; the attacker's retained foothold is.

choices.label: The portal blocks future logins until MFA is also reset.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: The attacker's MFA method stays registered. Password-recovery flows route through it. The attacker recovers the new password.

questions.prompt: The affected identity is a service account that other apps authenticate as. What does the lesson say?
choices: Bump to senior. A password reset on a service account has broader downstream impact; the senior coordinates with the customer's IT.

choices.label: Reset as normal. The playbook is the same.
choices.rationale: Downstream impact on dependent systems differs from a regular user account.

choices.label: Skip the reset for service accounts.
choices.rationale: The reset is still needed; coordination is the senior's job.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket for guidance.
choices.rationale: The senior is the right path, not Huntress support.

questions.explanation: Service-account password resets break dependent apps. The senior coordinates the timing with the customer's IT.

questions.prompt: Which is a complete verification check after the password and MFA reset step?

choices.label: Portal shows the action complete; nothing else needed.
choices.rationale: Action complete in the portal is not enough. Verify state and successful user sign-in.

choices: Portal shows password reset complete with current timestamp; MFA-methods list reflects the reset; new password is in the secure-handoff channel only; user can sign in successfully.

choices.label: Customer's IT manager confirms by email.
choices.rationale: Customer confirmation is not the platform-state verification.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours and check if anything broke.
choices.rationale: Wrong delay; verification is immediate.

questions.explanation: The full verification set: portal timestamp, MFA-methods state, secure-channel credential, and successful user sign-in.

questions.prompt: Texting the new password to the user's recovery phone number is a safe handoff method during a compromise response.
questions.explanation: The recovery phone number may have been changed by the attacker during MFA tampering. Sending the credential there delivers it to the attacker.

questions.prompt: What should you tell the user about MFA re-enrolment during the handoff call?
choices: After they sign in with the new password, they will be prompted to set up MFA again. Use their own phone, not a shared device. Prefer an authenticator app over SMS where possible.

choices.label: Skip MFA setup until next week so the user has time to think.
choices.rationale: Delays the response and weakens protection.

choices.label: Have the user share their MFA setup screen via screen-share so you can configure it.
choices.rationale: The user sets up their own MFA on their own device.

choices.label: Tell the user MFA is no longer required after a reset.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the platform. MFA is still required.

questions.explanation: Brief, practical guidance: own phone, authenticator app preferred, prompt appears on next sign-in.
